questions.question: Which act is the recent one to amend the tenth schedule of the constitution?
questions.explanation: Solu nbvcxtion: The 91st Amendment Act of 2003 made one change in the provisions of the Tenth Schedule. It omitted an exception provision i.e., disqualification on ground of defection not to apply in case of split.

questions.question: Proxy voting relates to ________?
options: Votes casted by MP's against their parties
questions.explanation: In 2003, the facility to opt to vote through proxy was provided to the service voters belonging to the Armed Forces and members belonging to a Force to which provisions of the Army Act apply. Such service voters who opt to vote through proxy must appoint a proxy in a prescribed format and intimate the Returning Officer of the constituency.

questions.question: Chairman of which among the following commissions is not an ex-officio member of National Human Rights Commission?
questions.explanation: In addition to the full-time members, the National Human Rights commission also has four ex-officio members - the chairmen of the National Commission for Minorities, the National Commission for SCs, the National Commission for STs and the National Commission for Women.

questions.question: The structural part of the constitution of India was derived from __________?
questions.explanation: The structural part of the Constitution, is to a significant extent, derived from the Government of India Act of 1935.

questions.question: Which among the following article dealing with fundamental rights upholds the ideal of Secularism in India?
questions.explanation: Article 25 to article 28 deals with freedom of religion. The term 'secular' too was added by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1976.

questions.question: Prior to its present status, which among the following states/Union territories was administered as an acquired territory?
questions.explanation: The territory of Puducherry comprises the former French establishments in India known as Puducherry, Karaikal, Mahe and Yanam. The French handed over this territory to India in 1954. Subsequently, it was administered as an 'acquired territory', till 1962 when it was made a union territory by the 14th Constitutional Amendment Act.

questions.question: Which among the following writs can be used by Supreme Court to order a public official to perform his duties?
questions.explanation: Mandamus means 'we command'. It is a command issued by the court to a public official asking him to perform his official duties that he has failed or refused to perform. It can also be issued against any public body, a corporation, an inferior court, a tribunal or government for the same purpose.

questions.question: Who among the following is an agent of President?
questions.explanation: Every union territory is administered by the President acting through an administrator appointed by him. An administrator i.e., Lieutenant Governor of a union territory is an agent of the President.

questions.question: Chief election commissioner can be removed in the same manner as ________?
questions.explanation: The chief election commissioner is provided with the security of tenure. He cannot be removed from his office except in same manner and on the same grounds as a judge of the Supreme Court.

questions.question: The National Commission for SCs presents its annual report to _______?
questions.explanation: The National Commission for SCs presents an annual report to the president. The President places all such reports before the Parliament, along with a memorandum explaining the action taken on the recommendations made by the Commission.

questions.question: Who among the following is charged with the responsibility to ensure attendance in the houses of parliament?
questions.explanation: Whip is charged with the responsibility of ensuring the attendance of his party members in large numbers and securing their support in favour of or against a particular issue. He regulates and monitors their behaviour in the Parliament. The members are supposed to follow the directives given by the whip.

questions.question: As per norms, there are totally how many parliamentary sessions in a year?
questions.explanation: There are usually three sessions in a year. 1. the Budget Session (February to May); 2. the Monsoon Session (July to September); and 3. the Winter Session (November to December).

questions.question: Who can prorogue the house of the parliament?
questions.explanation: President issues a notification for prorogation of the session. The President can also prorogue the House while in session.

questions.question: Which among the following type of parliamentary devices requires a written answer?
questions.explanation: An un-starred question, on the other hand, requires a written answer and hence, supplementary questions cannot follow. A starred question (distinguished by an asterisk) requires an oral answer and hence supplementary questions can follow. A short notice question is one that is asked by giving a notice of less than ten days. It is answered orally.

questions.question: Which among the following is NOT a part of Committees to Scrutinise and Control?
options: Committee on Welfare of SCs and STs
questions.explanation: Committee on Petitions forms a part of Committees to Inquire. Committees to Scrutinise and Control constitute of (a) Committee on Government Assurances (b) Committee on Subordinate Legislation (c) Committee on Papers Laid on the Table (d) Committee on Welfare of SCs and STs (e) Committee on Empowerment of Women (f) Joint Committee on Offices of Profit.

questions.question: Who can summon a Joint sitting of houses of parliament?
questions.explanation: The president can summon both the Houses to meet in a joint sitting for the purpose of deliberating and voting on the bill. It must be noted here that the provision of joint sitting is applicable to ordinary bills or financial bills only and not to money bills or Constitutional amendment bills.

questions.question: Which of the following disputes falls under the original jurisdiction of Supreme Court?
options: Dispute between centre and a state.
Cases involving violation of fundamental rights
Disputes relating to river water sharing
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court decides the disputes between different units of the Indian Federation. More elaborately, any dispute between: (a) the Centre and one or more states; or (b) the Centre and any state or states on one side and one or more states on the other; or (c) between two or more states. This falls under original jurisdiction of supreme court.

questions.question: What should be the minimum strength of state legislative councils?
questions.explanation: The maximum strength of the state legislative council is fixed at one-third of the total strength of the assembly and the minimum strength is fixed at 40.

questions.question: What is the normal duration of legislative assembly of Jammu & Kashmir?
questions.explanation: The term of the legislative assembly of Jammu and Kashmir is six years under its own state Constitution. For rest of the states in India, it is for 5 years.

questions.question: A judge of a High court can hold the office till he/she attains what age?
questions.explanation: Judge of a high court holds office until he attains the age of 62 years.

questions.question: Disputes relating to the election of members of parliament is dealt by ________?
questions.explanation: Disputes relating to the election of members of Parliament and state legislatures fall under the original jurisdiction of High Courts.

questions.question: Articles relating to President of India is provided in which part of the constitution?
questions.explanation: Articles 52 to 78 in Part V of the Constitution deal with the Union executive. It also includes President of India.

questions.question: The eleventh schedule dealing with panchayat has how many functional items in it?
questions.explanation: Eleventh Schedule contains the totally 29 functional items placed within the purview of panchayats raj system.

questions.question: Which authority conducts elections to urban local bodies?
questions.explanation: The superintendence, direction and control of the preparation of electoral rolls and the conduct of all elections to the municipalities shall be vested in the state election commission.

questions.question: Which amendment act is related to establishment of Municipalities?
questions.explanation: The system of urban government was constitutionalized through the 74th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992. This Act has added a new Part IX-A to the Constitution of India. It is entitled 'The Municipalities' and consists of provisions from Articles 243-P to 243-ZG. In addition, the act has also added a new Twelfth Schedule to the Constitution.

questions.question: National anthem was adopted in which year?
questions.explanation: Constituent Assembly adopted the national anthem on January 24, 1950.

questions.question: A person can acquire citizenship of India through which of the following methods?
questions.explanation: The Citizenship Act of 1955 prescribes five ways of acquiring citizenship, viz, birth, descent, registration, naturalisation and incorporation of territory.

questions.question: Which among the following rights guarantees right to constitutional remedies?
questions.explanation: Article 32 confers the right to constitutional remedies for the enforcement of the fundamental rights of an aggrieved citizen. The right to get the Fundamental Rights protected is in itself a fundamental right.

questions.question: Feature of concurrent list was borrowed from constitution of which country?
questions.explanation: Features borrowed from Australian Constitution are: Concurrent List, freedom of trade, commerce and inter-course, and joint sitting of the two Houses of Parliament.

questions.question: Which of the following feature of Indian constitution promotes the idea of welfare state?
questions.explanation: The Directive Principles constitute a very comprehensive economic, social and political programme for a modern democratic State. They aim at realising the high ideals of justice, liberty, equality and fraternity as outlined in the Preamble to the Constitution. They embody the concept of a 'welfare state' and not that of a 'police state', which existed during the colonial era. In brief, they seek to establish economic and social democracy in the country.
